verses.text: The Pharisees fault the disciples for eating with unwashen hands, holding 'the tradition of the elders.' Manmade rules. They prize their customs above God's law.
The Pharisees fault the disciples for eating with unwashen hands, holding 'the tradition of the elders.' Manmade rules. They prize their customs above God's law.
The Pharisees fault the disciples for eating with unwashen hands, holding 'the tradition of the elders.' Manmade rules. They prize their customs above God's law.
The Pharisees fault the disciples for eating with unwashen hands, holding 'the tradition of the elders.' Manmade rules. They prize their customs above God's law.
'Ye lay aside the commandment of God,' He says, 'to hold the tradition of men'; this people honours Him with lips, not heart. Empty worship. Tradition can crowd out true obedience.

By 'Corban' they free men from honouring parents, 'making the word of God of none effect.' Crafty evasion. Religious excuses cannot cancel plain duty.

Nothing from without can defile a man, but the things from within. The real source. Sin springs from the heart, not the hands.
Nothing from without can defile a man, but the things from within. The real source. Sin springs from the heart, not the hands.
Nothing from without can defile a man, but the things from within. The real source. Sin springs from the heart, not the hands.
What enters the belly is purged, not the heart; thus He makes all meats clean. Outward things. Food cannot touch the inner man.
What enters the belly is purged, not the heart; thus He makes all meats clean. Outward things. Food cannot touch the inner man.
What enters the belly is purged, not the heart; thus He makes all meats clean. Outward things. Food cannot touch the inner man.
From the heart come evil thoughts, murders, adulteries, pride; these defile. The heart's overflow. Defilement is bred within, not without.

A Greek woman of Syrophenicia begs Him for her demon-vexed daughter. Persistent plea. A Gentile mother seeks the mercy of Israel's Lord.
A Greek woman of Syrophenicia begs Him for her demon-vexed daughter. Persistent plea. A Gentile mother seeks the mercy of Israel's Lord.
A Greek woman of Syrophenicia begs Him for her demon-vexed daughter. Persistent plea. A Gentile mother seeks the mercy of Israel's Lord.
Let the children first be filled; she answers, the dogs eat the crumbs; 'for this saying' the devil is gone. Humble faith. Her lowly, clinging trust wins the blessing.

They bring a deaf man with a speech impediment; He sighs, touches him, and says, 'Ephphatha, Be opened.' Tender care. He deals personally and gently with the afflicted.

His ears open and tongue loosed; though charged silence, they publish it, 'He hath done all things well.' Overflowing praise. Mercy cannot be kept hidden.
